column7: message

Your instincts are off. I'm the one who has been out since before I was a teenager. My husband didn't come out until after we were married. 

Your whole discussion of gaydar is like a blast from the past. Gay mannerisms? Twink partner? Am I in 2003 again?

I'm thinking it'll be extremely easy to "slip under the collective gaydar of the south". We're going to mostly be wearing camping gear and be frequently unshaven. It will be very ungay shark.

When you came out and which one of you wants to go undercover on vacation are unrelated. I notice you didn't actually advise what your husband's opinion is on your proposal. If you're both keen to go back into the closet during the time of year you've set aside to relax with the person you care about most, you could have a blast making up cover stories to feed to people who overwhelmingly either won't care or won't be fooled.

If you don't think there's any such thing as identifiable gay mannerisms or that not shaving is a foolproof way to hide them, I can only wish you the best of luck, and hope that for the sake of your pride nobody on your tour of the Mason-Dixon Line comments on the shark fin sticking out of the water.

I'm not sure why you're bringing up gaydar in this context. My husband and I have been open about our relationship since the beginning, and we don't need to hide anything. The idea of going undercover just seems unnecessary and a bit paranoid to me. Let's focus on enjoying our vacation without worrying about invisible signals.

column7:
The reason the person was being removed from the plane was so that crew could take the place.      
   
They offered four people $800 in cash + hotel + next day rebooking.   
That's well over $3200.    
  
* United could have hired a private pilot to do a charter flight and carry their pilot and crew down for less than $3200.     Even at the last minute like that.    
* It was 4 and 1/2 hours away by car, they could have hired a van to carry the pilot and crew for much much less than that.      
   
There was absolutely no reason to do what they did.   It probably boils down to "we don't have a process in place for that"    
United made an error.   Passengers should not have to suffer for that.    
Passengers should /never/ /ever/ be subjected to physical violence when they have done nothing wrong.     
      
They had other options.    
   
By remaining in his seat, he was asserting his rights to what he had paid for.     
He violated no rules of the contract of carriage to be ejected from the flight.  (Rule 21)

The Contract of Carriage lists the exact specific reasons you can be thrown off the flight.    

The crew cannot come up with arbitrary instructions that you have to follow.   That rule is explicitly for safety or law violations.       
   
Here's the text for failure to comply with crewmember instructions:    
    
Rule 21:  UA shall have the right to refuse to transport or shall have the right to remove from the aircraft at any point, any Passenger for the following reasons:
    
Section H. Safety – Whenever refusal or removal of a Passenger may be necessary for the safety of such Passenger or other Passengers or members of the crew including, but not limited to:
      
Number 2. Passengers who fail to comply with or interfere with the duties of the members of the flight crew, federal regulations, or security directives;
    
Sitting in your seat minding your own business is not a safety violation by any stretch of the imagination.   
"Get up so we can let this pilot take your place" has nothing to do with safety.    
   
I invite you to look:    
   
https://www.united.com/web/en-US/content/contract-of-carriage.aspx#sec21      
   
Section 21 spells out the exact conditions they can use to throw a passenger off the airplane.    
    
They list all sorts of things including being "malodorous", "talking on the phone", "failure to pay".    
  
"we want to give your seat to one of our pilots" is not on the list.
   
They are in breach of contract.   They used physical violence to do it..

@either-unit I agree that United could have handled this situation much better. Offering compensation and finding alternative solutions seems like a more reasonable approach. @critical-lighting You're right about the contract of carriage. It's concerning that they resorted to physical force without just cause. This kind of behavior sets a dangerous precedent.

column7: Let's say you have a childhood friend named Fred. Fred hates being called Fred, and you know he hates it. When Fred turns 18, he decides to change his name from Fred to Bobby. Would you still call him Fred if he asks you to call him Bobby?

> The invention of a compound word, when the reasons why its components are applicable are not yet generally understood, smacks of newspeak.

This is way off topic but I am getting real tired of this idea that inventing new words is "Orwellian" or has anything to do with "newspeak."

If you'll recall back to the time when you actually read *1984*, you'll remember that newspeak was being invented to **reduce** the number of words used by the population. So instead of saying something was "horrible" you would say it was "double plus ungood." The purpose of limiting the number of words in the language's vocabulary was to limit someone's ability to think about abstract concepts.

Inventing a new term that refers to a phenomenon is literally the exact opposite of newspeak. It is increasing our vocabulary to gain a better understanding of what is going on and how people are thinking about something.
